turns.text: I've got three VC term sheets on my desk.
Need to pick the right partner for the long haul.
Valuation is one thing, but actual growth support is everything.
Firm A is offering a $5 million Series A at a $20 million pre-money.
But their liquidation preference is 1x participating.
That gives me pause, honestly.
Firm B's term sheet is cleaner: $4.5 million at $18 million pre.
They're offering a standard 1x non-participating preference.
Their partner has deep connections in our SaaS vertical, though.
Then there's Firm C.
They came in at a $22 million pre for $5.5 million.
But they want a board seat and aggressive anti-dilution protection.
I'm worried about control down the line.
My co-founder is leaning toward the highest valuation.
I'm more concerned with the partner's operational experience.
We need someone who's been through scaling to 100 employees.
Firm B's lead has that exact track record.
But is the lower valuation a strategic misstep?
Let's start by comparing the effective post-money ownership for each.
I need to see the full cap table impact first.
Okay, running the numbers for Firm A first.
Post-money would be $25 million with their $5 million.
That gives them 20% ownership on paper.
But with participating preferred, their take in a sale gets messy.
Let's model a $50 million exit scenario.
Firm B's post-money is $22.5 million.
They'd own exactly 20% too, for $4.5 million.
Non-participating is so much cleaner for the team.
Firm C's post-money is $27.5 million.
Their $5.5 million buys 20% as well.
But that anti-dilution could really hurt us in a down round.
My co-founder just texted—he's fixated on the extra $500k from C.
I think that's short-term thinking.
The partner from Firm B reached out again.
She offered to intro us to three potential enterprise customers next month.
That's tangible growth support.
Firm A's references were mixed on hands-on help.
One founder said they were mostly financial engineers.
Control is my non-negotiable.
Firm C's board seat demand plus the anti-dilution… that's a lot.
Let's model that $50M exit for Firm A's participating preferred.
They get their $5M back first.
Then they share the remaining $45M pro-rata.
That leaves the common pool much smaller.
For Firm B, it's just 20% of the whole $50M.
The team keeps way more.
That clarity is worth a slightly lower valuation now.
Firm C's anti-dilution is broad-based weighted average.
Still, it's a drag on future fundraising.
I need to talk my co-founder off the valuation ledge.
He's not seeing the long-term cap table erosion.
Firm B's customer intros could accelerate revenue by six months.
That's more valuable than an extra half-million today.
Okay, I'm scheduling a call with the Firm B partner.
I want to dig into her scaling playbook.
Specifically, how she handled our cash burn rate at this stage.
My co-founder agreed to join the call.
Good. He needs to hear the operational pitch directly.
I'm drafting a list of deal-breakers for the term sheet negotiation.
Board composition is top of that list.
Our call with Firm B is set for tomorrow.
I'm prioritizing board seat veto rights.
We need to keep control over hiring the first CRO.
My co-founder is now reviewing the full reference checks.
He's starting to see the value in the hands-on support.
I told Firm A we're leaning toward another offer.
They came back willing to drop the participating preference.
But it's too late—their lack of operational depth is clear.
Firm C is pushing hard on their 'higher valuation equals better optics'.
That's not what fuels real growth.
Our call with Firm B's partner was solid.
She mapped out a 24-month scaling plan with us.
My co-founder was finally convinced.
So we're moving forward with Firm B's term sheet.
Now we negotiate the final points.
We'll ask for standard information rights, not overly burdensome.
And we need a clear drag-along threshold.
I'm sending our markups to their counsel tonight.
Feeling good about this path.
Growth support over pure valuation was the right call.
Our counsel just sent back their redlines.
They accepted our board seat veto.
But they pushed back on the drag-along threshold.
We're holding firm at 60% for that.
The partner just emailed to finalize.
We're signing the term sheet tomorrow.
It feels like a true partnership.
Those customer intros are already scheduled.
My co-founder is thrilled.
He sees the growth engine now.
We dodged a bullet with those other terms.
Clarity on the cap table is priceless.
I'm glad we prioritized the right metrics.
Valuation isn't the only scorecard.
The real work starts next week.
But we've got the right partner for it.
Thanks for helping me think this through.
I'm signing off to prep for the close.
Feels like a solid foundation.
On to building.
